Exit code: 0 Wall time: 1.1 seconds Output: .\AGENTS.md:9:Designflow Backend is the main REST API for the Designflow PLM platform. It is a stateless Node.js + Express service backed by PostgreSQL (Sequelize ORM). Identity comes from a JWT on every request; session state does not exist. The service runs on Google Cloud Run and is reached exclusively through the BFF (`designflow-bff`), which terminates the user JWT and adds an OIDC service-to-service token before forwarding. The frontend is a separate Angular SPA in a sibling repository. For a full system diagram see `docs/architecture.md`. .\AGENTS.md:35:| Deploy / Docker / Cloud Build / Cloud Run / rollback | `AGENTS.md` (§13), `docs/deployment.md`, `docs/configuration.md` | `docs/development.md` | .\AGENTS.md:103:| `cloudbuild.yaml` | Google Cloud Build CI/CD definition | .\AGENTS.md:161:6. `Customer.getRFQStep` must fall back to the JWT `role` when no user/vendor row resolves, otherwise factory-row impersonation can crash Cloud Run with `Cannot read properties of null (reading 'level')`. .\AGENTS.md:202:| Change build or deploy | `cloudbuild.yaml`, `Dockerfile` | `config/env.js` (only if adding a new environment) | .\AGENTS.md:203:| Add environment variable | `.env.example`, `docs/configuration.md`, the relevant `cloudbuild.yaml` `--set-env-vars` or `--set-secrets` block | Hardcode into source | .\AGENTS.md:238:| `designflow-backend` (this repo) | Core REST API | Google Cloud Run | `{REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/{PROJECT}/{REPO}/{ENV}/backend:{BUILD_ID}-{SHA}` | .\AGENTS.md:248:Cloud Build substitution variables (`cloudbuild.yaml`): `_REGION`, `_SERVICE_NAME`, `_APP_ENV`, `_APP_NAME`, `_ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME`, `_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME`, `_PORT`, `_CPU`, `_MEMORY`, `_CONCURRENCY`, `_MAX_INSTANCES`. All passed at trigger time — none hardcoded. .\AGENTS.md:321:**Actually:** The BFF puts the OIDC service-to-service token in `Authorization` (so Cloud Run can read it) and the user JWT in `X-User-Authorization`. Cloud Run strips the OIDC token. This swap restores the user JWT to where `helpers/auth.js` expects it. **Without this swap, no route handler sees the user.** .\AGENTS.md:335:### `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` not in `--set-env-vars` in `cloudbuild.yaml` .\AGENTS.md:407:| `DATABASE_URL` / `DB_*` | PostgreSQL connection | `.env.` / Cloud Run secrets | .\AGENTS.md:421:**Never put secrets in `--set-env-vars`.** That flag is a full replacement on every deploy — any value not listed is deleted. Secrets go in `--set-secrets` (Cloud Run Secret Manager references), which persist independently. .\AGENTS.md:429:- **CI/CD:** Google Cloud Build (`cloudbuild.yaml`) — no GitHub Actions .\AGENTS.md:431:- **Runtime:** Google Cloud Run (managed, serverless, stateless) .\AGENTS.md:433:- **Rollback:** Cloud Run revision traffic split via `gcloud run services update-traffic` .\AGENTS.md:435:- **Branch policy:** `sandbox-albert` → sandbox Cloud Run service. Never merge directly to `develop` — PRs only. .\AGENTS.md:467:**What happened:** `POST /auth/azure` returned 401. Cloud Run logged: `The request was not authorized to invoke this service. The access token could not be verified.` .\AGENTS.md:469:**Root cause:** The BFF uses `CORE_BACKEND_URL` as both the proxy target and the OIDC token `aud` claim. Cloud Run validates `aud` against the service's `.run.app` URL. Custom domains route traffic correctly via Cloud Run domain mapping **but do not work as OIDC audiences**. When `CORE_BACKEND_URL` was set to a custom domain, every OIDC token was rejected. .\AGENTS.md:471:**Recovery:** Update `CORE_BACKEND_URL` (and `ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL`, etc.) on the BFF Cloud Run service config and `designflow-bff/env/.env.*` to the `.run.app` service URL. .\AGENTS.md:475:# Check current BFF env vars on Cloud Run .\AGENTS.md:489:**What happened:** A secret added manually to the Cloud Run service via the console disappeared after the next `cloudbuild.yaml` deploy. .\AGENTS.md:493:**Recovery:** Move secrets to Secret Manager. Reference them in `cloudbuild.yaml` via `--set-secrets` (e.g. `--set-secrets=OPENROUTER_API_KEY=openrouter-api-key:latest`), which persists independently across deploys. .\AGENTS.md:509:**Prevention (in place):** `client_encoding: 'UTF8'` added to `dialectOptions` in both `designflow-backend/models/db.js` and `designflow-data-syncing/models/db.js`. This forces UTF-8 on the connection so the app can't introduce new mojibake; it does **not** retro-fix existing data. .\AGENTS.md:519:| ready (uncommitted) | Factory bulk offer upload — `helpers/rfqBulkImport.js` (new), `helpers/digitalocean.js` (`uploadBufferToSpaces`), `helpers/multer.js` (`multerMemory`), `models/lib.model.js` (`getRFQFactoryTemplate`, `importFactoryRFQ`, `describeImportRowError`), `controllers/main.controller.js` (SYS-104/SYS-105), `routes/lib.router.js` (2 new routes). 2026-06-11 also clarified `rfqCopyMerchGroupGuard.js` blocked-copy messaging. | Commit + `git push origin sandbox-albert` when Albert approves. See `HANDOFF.md`; rerun full `npm test` before final push if the branch stays open. | .\cloudbuild.yaml:19:# 🚀 Deploy the image to Google Cloud Run .\cloudbuild.yaml:26: - ${_SERVICE_NAME} .\cloudbuild.yaml:36: - --platform=${_PLATFORM} #Specifies to use the fully managed Google Cloud Run platform (as opposed to Anthos/GKE-based Cloud Run) .\cloudbuild.yaml:70: _SERVICE_NAME: $$_SERVICE_NAME .\cloudbuild.yaml:73: _ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME: $$_ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME .\cloudbuild.yaml:91: _IMAGE: ${_REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/${PROJECT_ID}/${_ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME}/${_APP_ENV}/${_APP_NAME}:${BUILD_ID}-${COMMIT_SHA} .\CLAUDE.md:1:# CLAUDE.md — Claude Code notes for designflow-backend .\CLAUDE.md:8:- Active branch: `sandbox-albert` .\CLAUDE.md:9:- Commit and push only to `sandbox-albert` .\CLAUDE.md:18:- Allowed: read/edit repo files, run `npm` scripts, use `git add`, `git commit`, and `git push origin sandbox-albert` .\CLAUDE.md:20:- SSH is not the normal deployment path. Deployment is Cloud Build -> Docker -> Cloud Run after branch push; use `gcloud` only for explicit rollback or incident triage. .\CLAUDE.md:29:Run the relevant tests before committing. `start:sandbox-albert` does not run tests before starting `nodemon`. .\HANDOFF.md:9:A vendor-only "Upload Offers" feature was fully implemented across both `designflow-backend` .\HANDOFF.md:11:`sandbox-albert`. .\HANDOFF.md:38:- All changes are on `sandbox-albert`, **unstaged** (no `git add`, no `git commit`) .\HANDOFF.md:39:- `develop` was merged into `sandbox-albert` again on 2026-06-11; local uncommitted work was preserved .\HANDOFF.md:56:git push origin sandbox-albert .\docs\architecture.md:159:1. Reads `Authorization: Bearer ` header. For Cloud Run requests coming through the BFF, `index.js` first middleware swaps `x-user-authorization` → `authorization` (the BFF puts the OIDC token in `authorization` for Cloud Run; the user JWT travels in `x-user-authorization` and is restored before any route handler runs) .\docs\configuration.md:7:In production (Cloud Run) there is no `.env` file — variables are injected by Cloud Build via `--set-secrets` (GCP Secret Manager) and `--set-env-vars`. .\docs\configuration.md:12:cp .env.example .env.sandbox-albert .\docs\configuration.md:42:| `NODE_OPTIONS` | `--set-env-vars` | Node flags for the Cloud Run container; currently `--max-old-space-size=896` to cap the V8 heap below the instance memory limit | .\docs\configuration.md:55:**Critical:** Do not add secrets to `--set-env-vars` in `cloudbuild.yaml`. Secrets belong in `--set-secrets`, which persists independently across deployments. .\docs\configuration.md:69:Then add to `cloudbuild.yaml` `--set-secrets` block: .\docs\deployment.md:9: → Step 3: gcloud run deploy → Cloud Run .\docs\deployment.md:13:No GitHub Actions. The access token could not be verified.` .\docs\deployment.md:225:**What broke and why:** Microsoft SSO itself was not broken — Azure AD authenticated the user correctly. The failure was in the next step: the BFF proxying the user's Azure token to the backend. The BFF attaches a Google Cloud OIDC token to every backend request so Cloud Run's infrastructure layer will accept the call (this is separate from user auth — it is Google Cloud service-to-service authentication). That Google OIDC token had the wrong `audience` claim because the BFF's `CORE_BACKEND_URL` was set to the custom domain `https://api.core.alsand.designflow.app`. Cloud Run validates OIDC token audiences against the service's `.run.app` URL only — custom domain URLs do not work as OIDC audiences, even when they are correctly mapped to the service via Cloud Run domain mappings. .\docs\deployment.md:229:**Fix:** Updated all four `*_BACKEND_URL` env vars in all three BFF Cloud Run services to `.run.app` service URLs: .\docs\deployment.md:251:docker build --build-arg NODE_ENV=sandbox-albert -t designflow-frontend:local . .\docs\development.md:34:npm run build:sandbox-albert .\docs\development.md:42:$env:NODE_ENV='sandbox-albert' .\docs\development.md:77:docker build --build-arg NODE_ENV=production -t designflow-frontend:local . .\docs\development.md:78:docker run -p 8080:80 designflow-frontend:local .\src\app\pages\editor\ai-settings\ai-settings.component.ts:66: ? Builds are triggered automatically by Cloud Build triggers configured in the Google Cloud Console. The trigger watches a specific branch and fires on push.

- `sandbox-albert` branch → sandbox Cloud Build trigger → `_APP_ENV=sandbox-albert` → `build:sandbox-albert`
- `develop` branch → develop trigger → `_APP_ENV=develop` → `build:develop`
- `main` / `master` branch → production trigger → `_APP_ENV=production` → `build:production`

Each trigger has its own substitution variables set in the Cloud Console (not in the YAML file).

## Docker image

**Build stages:**

1. **Builder** (`node:lts`):
   - `WORKDIR /app`
   - `yarn install --frozen-lockfile --production=false`
   - `yarn test` — **tests must pass or the build fails**
   - `yarn build:${NODE_ENV}` — Angular build with environment substitution
   - Output: `dist/browser/`

2. **Runtime** (`macbre/nginx-brotli:1.19.10`):
   - Copies `nginx.conf`
   - Copies `dist/browser/` to `/usr/share/nginx/html/`
   - Exposes port 80
   - Brotli-enabled nginx serves the SPA

**Image tag format:**
```
{REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/{PROJECT_ID}/{ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME}/{APP_ENV}/{APP_NAME}:{BUILD_ID}-{COMMIT_SHA}
```

Example:
```
us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/my-project/designflow/production/designflow-frontend:abc123-d4e5f6a7
```

## Cloud Run service

The image is deployed to Google Cloud Run (managed platform, not GKE). Cloud Run scales to zero when idle. Configuration:

| Parameter | Controlled by | Default (from cloudbuild.yaml) |
|---|---|---|
| CPU | `_CPU` trigger variable | `1` |
| Memory | `_MEMORY` trigger variable | `512Mi` |
| Concurrency | `_CONCURRENCY` trigger variable | `80` |
| Max instances | `_MAX_INSTANCES` trigger variable | `10` |
| Auth | `--allow-unauthenticated` | Public (no Cloud IAM auth) |

Runtime environment variables are not injected into the frontend container — all config is baked in at build time.

## Environments

| Environment | Branch | API target | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `sandbox-albert` | `sandbox-albert` | `api.alsand.designflow.app` | Albert's personal dev/test environment |
| `staging` / `develop` | `develop` | `api.sandbox.designflow.app` | Shared staging environment |
| `production` | main branch | `api.designflow.app` | Live production |

## Rollback

steps:
  # Step 1: Build Docker Image
  - id: Build
    name: gcr.io/cloud-builders/docker
    args:
      - build
      - -t
      - ${_IMAGE}
      - --build-arg=NODE_ENV=${_APP_ENV}
      - --build-arg=COMMIT_SHA=${COMMIT_SHA}
      - .

  # Step 2: Push Docker Image to Artifact Registry
  - id: Push
    name: gcr.io/cloud-builders/docker
    args:
      - push
      - ${_IMAGE}
    waitFor:
      - Build

  # Step 3: Deploy to Cloud Run
  - id: Deploy
    name: gcr.io/google.com/cloudsdktool/cloud-sdk
    entrypoint: gcloud
    args:
      - run
      - deploy
      - ${_SERVICE_NAME}
      - --image=${_IMAGE}
      - --region=${_REGION}
      - --port=${_PORT}
      - --cpu=${_CPU}
      - --memory=${_MEMORY}
      - --concurrency=${_CONCURRENCY}
      - --max-instances=${_MAX_INSTANCES}
      - --allow-unauthenticated
      - --platform=${_PLATFORM}
      - --service-account=${_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME}
    waitFor:
      - Push

substitutions:
  _REGION: $$_REGION
  _SERVICE_NAME: $$_SERVICE_NAME
  _APP_ENV: $$_APP_ENV
  _APP_NAME: $$_APP_NAME
  _ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME: $$_ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME
  _SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME: $$_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME
  _PORT: $$_PORT
  _CPU: $$_CPU
  _MEMORY: $$_MEMORY
  _CONCURRENCY: $$_CONCURRENCY
  _MAX_INSTANCES: $$_MAX_INSTANCES
  _PLATFORM: $$_PLATFORM
  _IMAGE: ${_REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/${PROJECT_ID}/${_ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME}/${_APP_ENV}/${_APP_NAME}:${BUILD_ID}-${COMMIT_SHA}

options:
  logging: CLOUD_LOGGING_ONLY

images:

Update only the Markdown files that need durable knowledge from this specific session.

Goal

Record what this session learned or changed so future developers and future AI sessions do not have to rediscover it.

Focus on:

Information from this session that will still matter later.

Idiosyncrasies that may look wrong but are intentional.

Mistakes, dead ends, or assumptions from this session that future sessions should avoid.

Architecture, data flow, deployment, configuration, or workflow changes.

New operational steps, rollback notes, feature flags, identifiers, limits, or dependencies.

Any temporary/incomplete work that needs a handoff.


Rules

Derive every doc update from code, config, migrations, workflows, commands run, or verified session findings.

Do not document guesses as facts. Mark unknowns clearly and say how to verify them.

Do not add secrets, tokens, passwords, private keys, or credential values.

Do not duplicate large explanations across multiple docs. Put the short routing/context note in AGENTS.md only if future AI sessions need to see it early; put topic detail in the relevant doc under docs/.

If the change does not require documentation updates, say so explicitly in the final report.


What To Update

AGENTS.md: only for high-signal guidance future AI sessions must see quickly, including new quirks, critical warnings, task routing, identifiers, or "do not repeat this mistake" notes.

HANDOFF.md: create or update only if work is unfinished, blocked, partially deployed, or requires continuation context. Delete it only when the unfinished work it describes is truly complete.

Topic docs under docs/: update the specific doc for the affected area, such as architecture, deployment, configuration, schema, worker logic, PopSG, Helper, Seafile, auth, bulk jobs, or known quirks.

README.md: update only if the quick-start or top-level orientation changed.

CLAUDE.md: update only for Claude Code-specific workflow rules. General AI/developer guidance belongs in AGENTS.md.


Required Session Note Shape

When adding session-derived knowledge, prefer concise entries like:

### [Short decision or warning name]

What changed:
[One or two sentences.]

Why:
[Constraint, incident, bug, user decision, or operational reason.]

Future sessions should:
[Concrete instruction: what to do, what to avoid, where to verify.]

For unfinished work, include:

## [Workstream name]

Status:
[done / partial / blocked / not started]

Done:
[Verified completed items.]

Next action:
[Exact next step.]

Risks / watchouts:
[Anything future sessions must not miss.]

Verification Before Final Response

Docs changed are limited to files that actually needed updates.

New claims match the repository or verified session findings.

No secrets were added.

HANDOFF.md exists only if continuation context is needed.

Any new quirk or warning explains why it exists and what would break if ignored.


Final Report

Reply with:

## Documentation Updates
| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `path/to/file.md` | Short summary |

## Handoff
- `HANDOFF.md`: present/absent
- Reason: ...

## Verification
- Docs verified against session findings: yes/no
- Secrets added: yes/no
- Unknowns remaining: ...

- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.

Custom domain mappings do not work as OIDC audiences in practice, even though they appear in Cloud Run domain mapping configuration. .\AGENTS.md:148:**Looks like:** A non-standard header. Why not just use `Authorization`? .\AGENTS.md:150:**Actually:** The BFF puts the Google OIDC token in `Authorization` (Cloud Run reads this for auth). The user's Designflow JWT goes to `X-User-Authorization`. The backend's first middleware in `index.js` swaps it back: if `x-user-authorization` is present, it overwrites `authorization` before any route handler runs. .\AGENTS.md:152:**Why:** Cloud Run's auth layer reads `Authorization`. If the user JWT were there, Cloud Run would reject it (wrong signature). Cloud Run logged: `The request was not authorized to invoke this service. The access token could not be verified.` .\AGENTS.md:210:**What broke and why:** Microsoft SSO itself was fine — Azure AD authenticated the user correctly. The failure was in the next step: the BFF proxying the user's Azure token to the backend. The BFF attaches a Google Cloud OIDC token to every backend request so Cloud Run accepts it (see §1). That Google OIDC token had the wrong `audience` claim because `CORE_BACKEND_URL` was set to the custom domain (`https://api.core.*.designflow.app`). Cloud Run validates OIDC token audiences against the service's `.run.app` URL only — custom domains are not accepted, even when they correctly route traffic to the service. .\AGENTS.md:212:**Why it appeared suddenly:** The BFF was redeployed (revision 00007, 2026-05-25) adding the OIDC token attachment code (`src/backendProxy.js`). Before that revision, the BFF sent no Authorization header to the backend, causing a different error (`Empty Authorization header`). The OIDC audience was never validated before because OIDC was never used before. .\AGENTS.md:214:**Fix:** Updated all four `*_BACKEND_URL` env vars in all three BFF Cloud Run services to `.run.app` service URLs via `gcloud run services update --update-env-vars`. payload.message:
Done. Qwen direct is now wired up in sandbox.

What I changed/fixed:
- Granted the backend Cloud Run service account access to Secret Manager secret `DASHSCOPE_API_KEY`.
- Forced a new backend Cloud Run revision so the new secret is actually mounted.
- Verified the backend is running the commit with the Alibaba model-list fix.
- Redeployed/verified the sandbox frontend.
- Switched sandbox AI settings to:
  - `ai-api = qwen-direct`
  - `ai-model = qwen/qwen-plus`

Verification:
- Live BFF route now returns Alibaba models from DashScope: `145` models, including `qwen/qwen3.7-plus`, `qwen/qwen-plus`, etc.
- Live Qwen chat smoke test succeeded with `qwen/qwen-plus`; response was `ok`.
- Usage telemetry came back with cache stats, including `prompt_cache_hit_rate_pct`.

Your key belongs only in GCP Secret Manager as `DASHSCOPE_API_KEY`. Do not put it in the frontend or repo. I did not print or expose the secret value.

If your browser still shows OpenRouter models, hard refresh the sandbox tab. The saved backend setting is now Qwen Direct, so AI Settings should load Alibaba Qwen models by default.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

Fixed. The failed backend build was not a code build failure; Docker build and image push succeeded. It failed at Cloud Run deploy because `DASHSCOPE_API_KEY` did not exist yet when build `89494709...` tried to create the revision.

I reran the backend trigger after the secret existed and IAM was fixed:

- New build: `11a8cb84-fa0c-4562-aa69-ef8041e06160`
- Status: `SUCCESS`
- Commit: `23aa3fd`
- Cloud Run revision: `popcre-albert-backend-sandbox-00100-wdq`
- Traffic: `100%`
- Image: the successful build image for `23aa3fd`

I also verified the live BFF route again: `qwen-direct` returns `145` Alibaba models, starting with `qwen/qwen3.7-max-2026-06-08`, `qwen/qwen3.7-plus-2026-05-26`, and `qwen/qwen3.7-plus`.

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

payload.message: The statistics exist, but they are not global dashboards and they are not in AI Settings. They render inside the duty-rate AI dialog once there is `lastAiUsage` from a classification call. That may be why it feels like they disappeared.

content.text: The statistics exist, but they are not global dashboards and they are not in AI Settings. They render inside the duty-rate AI dialog once there is `lastAiUsage` from a classification call. That may be why it feels like they disappeared.

payload.message:
They are in the RFQ duty-rate AI classifier, not in AI Settings as a global dashboard.

Current locations:

| Place | What you get |
|---|---|
| [helpers/cache-metrics.js](D:/repos/dflow/designflow-backend/helpers/cache-metrics.js:18) | Normalizes provider usage into `prompt_cache_hit_tokens`, `prompt_cache_miss_tokens`, `prompt_cache_creation_tokens`, `prompt_cache_hit_rate_pct`, and `cache_stats`. |
| [helpers/qwen.js](D:/repos/dflow/designflow-backend/helpers/qwen.js:193) | Logs Qwen session-cache metrics after Alibaba returns usage. |
| [helpers/deepseek.js](D:/repos/dflow/designflow-backend/helpers/deepseek.js:67) | Logs DeepSeek automatic-prefix cache metrics. |
| [duty_rate_dialog.component.html](D:/repos/dflow/designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/rfq/duty_rate_dialog/duty_rate_dialog.component.html:101) | Shows the visible cache stats panel in the RFQ AI dialog. |
| [duty_rate_dialog.component.ts](D:/repos/dflow/designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/rfq/duty_rate_dialog/duty_rate_dialog.component.ts:305) | Computes the UI summary from cumulative session usage. |

The UI panel currently shows:

- Cache hit rate
- Cached input tokens
- New input tokens
- Cache writes
- Context cached %
- Prompt total
- Completion tokens
- Total tokens
- Reasoning tokens, when present
- Estimated cost
- Estimated cache savings
- Warning when provider cache telemetry is unavailable

The backend also returns these fields on each AI response under `usage`:

```json
{
  "prompt_cache_hit_tokens": 0,
  "prompt_cache_miss_tokens": 49,
  "prompt_cache_creation_tokens": 0,
  "prompt_cache_hit_rate_pct": 0,
  "prompt_cache_telemetry_available": true,
  "cache_stats": {
    "prompt_tokens": 49,
    "hit_tokens": 0,
    "miss_tokens": 49,
    "creation_tokens": 0,
    "hit_rate_pct": 0,
    "telemetry_available": true
  }
}
```

The missing piece: there is no persistent/global cache analytics page yet. The stats are per dialog session, held in frontend memory as `sessionUsage`, and disappear when the dialog closes. AI Settings shows cache strategy/eligibility/pricing, but not historical hit rate because it does not store or query past AI calls.

So: the diagnostics exist for live RFQ AI calls, but we do not yet have an AI Settings dashboard or backend persistence for “last 24h / last 100 calls / by provider / by model” cache performance.

content.text: The backend uses Sequelize model files directly rather than a visible migrations folder, so I’m checking how tables are introduced and whether startup syncs models or expects existing schema. That decides whether this should be a true table model or a JSON aggregate in `app_settings`.

payload.message: There’s explicit repo guidance to add `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` startup migrations and tests for new tables. I’m going to implement this as an `ai_cache_events` table, because an append-only event table lets the admin panel filter by window/provider/model and avoids lossy global counters.

content.text: There’s explicit repo guidance to add `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` startup migrations and tests for new tables. I’m going to implement this as an `ai_cache_events` table, because an append-only event table lets the admin panel filter by window/provider/model and avoids lossy global counters.

payload.message: Before editing, one design choice: the aggregate will track provider-returned telemetry only. If a provider silently caches but does not return cache counters, the admin panel will show that as “telemetry unavailable” rather than pretending zero cache usage.

payload.message:
Implemented the aggregate admin view.

What changed:
- Backend now stores one AI cache telemetry event per AI response in `ai_cache_events`.
- Admin endpoint added: `GET /api/admin/ai-cache-stats?window=24h&feature=hts-classification`.
- HTS classification calls are tagged as `hts-classification`.
- AI Settings now shows aggregate cache diagnostics for `24h`, `7d`, and `30d`.

The admin panel now shows:
- Hit rate
- Telemetry coverage
- Cached context %
- Calls
- Sessions
- Prompt tokens
- Cached input
- New input
- Cache writes
- Completion tokens
- Total tokens
- Avg prompt per call
- Avg cached per call
- Breakdown by provider
- Breakdown by model

Committed and pushed:
- Backend: `1a3f718 feat(ai): aggregate cache telemetry`
- Frontend: `2817bfe7 feat(ai): show aggregate cache utilization`

Deployed:
- Backend Cloud Build `05483453...`: success, revision `popcre-albert-backend-sandbox-00101-8l7`
- Frontend Cloud Build `27617ed6...`: success, revision `popcre-albert-frontend-sandbox-00193-5mh`

Verification:
- Backend tests passed.
- Frontend sandbox build passed with only existing bundle-size/side-effect warnings.
- Live admin stats endpoint works.
- A non-HTS `smoke-test` telemetry call recorded and aggregated correctly.
- Real HTS classification stats start accumulating from this deployment forward, so the dashboard will be empty until new classifications run.

Note: frontend still has unrelated local RFQ/tech-pack-warning changes that I did not touch or commit.

content.text:
1) and these statistics are per API provider, right?
2) it's takes quote long for a classification and we don't have hundreds of products. i was thinking we could add a RAG to look up the HTS number and MFN rate locally and if we get a 90% confidence match, not query cross. but the system would have to be smart enough to calculate the confidence number. and to know how to calculate the confidence number based on the 2 descriptions (the new project requesting classification and the one in the RAG). And for each ai classification that we actually go out to CROSS for we'd have to ingest more data than just what is applicable to that product, it would have to understand similar products that would fall under that. It would have to keep in it's database other attributes that  would make a product fall under this HTS number even if the one we ai classifying now doesn't have those attributes. As crazy as it sounds it's kind of like building our own internal CROSS system, but for the 100-150 products that we do instead of the tens of thousands that CROSS has.
Is this too ambitious / unrealistic?

payload.message:
1) and these statistics are per API provider, right?
2) it's takes quote long for a classification and we don't have hundreds of products. i was thinking we could add a RAG to look up the HTS number and MFN rate locally and if we get a 90% confidence match, not query cross. but the system would have to be smart enough to calculate the confidence number. and to know how to calculate the confidence number based on the 2 descriptions (the new project requesting classification and the one in the RAG). And for each ai classification that we actually go out to CROSS for we'd have to ingest more data than just what is applicable to that product, it would have to understand similar products that would fall under that. It would have to keep in it's database other attributes that  would make a product fall under this HTS number even if the one we ai classifying now doesn't have those attributes. As crazy as it sounds it's kind of like building our own internal CROSS system, but for the 100-150 products that we do instead of the tens of thousands that CROSS has.
Is this too ambitious / unrealistic?

1. Yes. The aggregate stats are broken down per provider and per model.

In AI Settings, the new dashboard includes:
- overall totals
- `by_provider`
- `by_model`
- `by_strategy`

So you can see Qwen vs DeepSeek vs OpenRouter separately, plus which model is actually getting cache hits. The endpoint also stores `api_backend`, `provider`, `model`, `cache_strategy`, and `feature` for every AI response.

2. Not crazy. Ambitious, yes. Unrealistic, no, as long as we build it as a decision-support layer, not as an instant replacement for CROSS.

What you’re describing is basically an internal classification memory:

- Store prior classified products.
- Store HTS code, MFN rate, reasoning, material, function, construction, use case, product category, exclusions, confidence, and source.
- When a new product comes in, compare it to prior examples.
- If similarity is very high and the relevant attributes line up, skip or reduce AI/CROSS work.
- If similarity is medium, use the match as context for AI.
- If similarity is low, go through full AI + CROSS lookup.
- Every confirmed classification improves the internal library.

The hard part is not RAG itself. The hard part is confidence.

A naive embedding similarity score is not enough. Two descriptions can be textually similar but legally different for HTS. Example: “ceramic decorative bowl” vs “ceramic dinnerware bowl” can be close semantically but classify differently depending on use. So confidence has to combine multiple signals:

- semantic similarity between descriptions
- normalized product type match
- material match
- use/function match
- construction/features match
- exclusions or red-flag attributes
- same HTS heading/subheading history
- whether prior examples had authoritative CROSS or human confirmation
- freshness of MFN rate data
- whether the new item contains attributes not present in the matched precedent

The right architecture would be staged:

1. **Internal memory only**
   Save every AI/CROSS classification result with structured attributes and final HTS/MFN.

2. **Similarity suggestions**
   Show “similar prior classifications” to the user/AI, but still run the normal flow.

3. **Confidence scoring**
   Add deterministic scoring: material/product/function match, embedding similarity, same HTS family, confirmed source weighting.

4. **AI-assisted verification**
   Ask the model: “Does this prior classification apply to this new product? Identify differences that would change HTS.” This is cheaper than full classification.

5. **Skip CROSS only above a high threshold**
   Something like: same product family + same material/function + no red flags + prior human/CROSS-confirmed classification + score >= 90.

So: not too ambitious if we treat it as a growing internal precedent system. It would be risky if we let “90% embedding match” automatically classify products. The system needs a conservative gate and should explain why it trusts a match.

For your product volume, this is actually a good fit. With only 100-150 recurring product types, you may get useful coverage quickly.

content.text:
1) maybe the RAG should record what DOES fall under that HTS in addition to what DOESN'T.
2) the key to success will be how well the system records other attributes that fall under the same HTS that don't necessarily apply to the product being queried at that moment.
3) i think in both new ai classifications (that actually queried CROSS) and RAG determinations the HTS number and rate should show the ruling numbers that led the model to make it's HTS determination and the ruling numbers are hyperlinks that take the user to the actual page of that ruling on https://rulings.cbp.gov/home

payload.message:
1) maybe the RAG should record what DOES fall under that HTS in addition to what DOESN'T.
2) the key to success will be how well the system records other attributes that fall under the same HTS that don't necessarily apply to the product being queried at that moment.
3) i think in both new ai classifications (that actually queried CROSS) and RAG determinations the HTS number and rate should show the ruling numbers that led the model to make it's HTS determination and the ruling numbers are hyperlinks that take the user to the actual page of that ruling on https://rulings.cbp.gov/home

Yes. That is exactly the right direction.

The RAG should not just store “this product got HTS X.” It should store an internal HTS precedent profile:

- **What falls under this HTS**
  Materials, forms, functions, construction details, dimensions, use cases, marketing language, product families, examples from rulings.

- **What does not fall under this HTS**
  Exclusions, nearby-but-different products, attributes that push the item to another heading, examples of rejected alternatives.

- **Why**
  The legal/classification logic: essential character, principal use, material dominance, decorative vs utilitarian, set vs single article, etc.

- **Authority**
  The CBP ruling numbers that support the determination, with links to `https://rulings.cbp.gov/home`.

For success, the system needs to extract broader classification rules from each CROSS session, not just facts about the current product. When it queries CROSS, it should ask AI to summarize:

- “Attributes present in this item that support HTS X”
- “Other attributes from similar rulings that also support HTS X”
- “Attributes that would exclude the item from HTS X”
- “Similar HTS codes considered and why they were rejected”
- “Ruling numbers relied on”

Then when RAG makes a future determination, the UI should show:

- HTS code
- MFN rate
- confidence
- matched internal precedent
- applicable included attributes
- missing-but-compatible attributes
- exclusion checks passed
- ruling numbers used, as hyperlinks

For the ruling links, we should store ruling IDs like `N345678`, `HQ H123456`, etc. The UI can render them as links into CBP rulings. We’ll need to verify the exact URL format CBP supports for direct ruling pages before implementing, but the stored canonical value should be the ruling number either way.

I’d model it less like “RAG over product descriptions” and more like an internal **HTS knowledge base**:

```text
HTS code
MFN rate
included attributes
excluded attributes
supporting rulings
contrasting rulings
known product examples
confidence rules
last verified date
human confirmed?
```

That makes the 90% confidence score much more defensible, because it is not just “the sentence sounds similar.” It becomes “the required inclusion attributes match, no exclusion attributes fired, and the determination is backed by these rulings.”

content.text: the text of the rulings are tiny. maybe we should record the entire text of every ruling we read

payload.message: the text of the rulings are tiny. maybe we should record the entire text of every ruling we read

Yes, I think we should store the full ruling text.

For CBP rulings specifically, that is probably the right move because:

- The texts are small.
- They are authoritative.
- The wording matters for classification logic.
- Full text lets us re-extract better structured attributes later as our prompt/schema improves.
- It avoids losing nuance when the first extraction misses something.

But I would not rely on full text alone. I’d store both:

1. **Raw ruling record**
   - ruling number
   - ruling date
   - source URL
   - full ruling text
   - HTS codes mentioned
   - product descriptions
   - country/origin if present
   - last fetched timestamp

2. **Structured classification intelligence**
   - falls-under attributes
   - does-not-fall-under attributes
   - material/function/use/construction signals
   - reasons this HTS applied
   - rejected/contrasting HTS codes
   - confidence impact
   - product family tags
   - extracted examples

3. **Search/RAG representation**
   - embeddings over the full ruling text
   - embeddings over extracted product examples
   - embeddings over normalized “classification rule” summaries
   - maybe per-HTS aggregate summaries generated from multiple rulings

The full ruling text is the source of truth. The structured fields are the working memory that makes the system fast and explainable.

For UI, every AI/RAG result should be able to say:

> HTS: 1234.56.7890  
> MFN: 5.3%  
> Supported by rulings: N123456, HQ H987654  
> Why: similar material/function/use pattern; no exclusion attributes matched.

And those ruling numbers should hyperlink to CBP.

One important implementation detail: if we store every full ruling we read, we should also store whether it was merely “retrieved” vs “relied on.” The model may read 10 rulings but base the answer mainly on 2. That distinction matters for trust.

payload.message: I found the CBP direct ruling URL pattern while drafting: rulings live at `https://rulings.cbp.gov/ruling/{RULING_NUMBER}`.
